original_abstract: Developing lead-free relaxors that simultaneously exhibit high capacitive energy density ($W_{rec}$) and superior storage efficiency ($\eta$) remains a critical challenge for advancing pulsed power technologies. While local compositional heterogeneity is known to drive the formation of complex polar structures in solid solution relaxors, its specific role in optimizing energy storage performance is frequently underestimated. In this study, we demonstrate that a remarkable $W_{rec}$ of 15.2 J cm⁻³ and an ultrahigh $\eta$ of 91% can be achieved by engineering local chemical clustering within $Bi_{0.5}Na_{0.5}TiO_3$-$BaTiO_3$-based relaxors. Utilizing a three-dimensional atomistic model derived from neutron and X-ray total scattering via the reverse Monte Carlo method, we reveal the existence of sub-nanometer clusters of Bi, Na, and Ba. These clusters harbor distinct polar displacements, validating predictions from density functional theory calculations. This local architecture fosters a polar state characterized by miniature clusters and significant fluctuations in both the magnitude and direction of unit-cell polar vectors. Macroscopically, these features translate to enhanced high-field polarizability, suppressed hysteresis, and elevated breakdown strength. Notably, this optimized polar structure leads to a unique field-enhanced efficiency, exceptional thermal and fatigue stability, and superior discharge capacity. This work underscores how hidden local chemical ordering profoundly influences polarization dynamics and offers a promising pathway for designing next-generation dielectric energy storage materials.
humanized_abstract: Energy storage capacitors made of lead-free relaxor materials are required for present and future developments of pulse power technologies that have to withstand very short time periods. They need therefore not only high value of the recoverable stored charge energy (W rec) but also a high energy storage efficiency (η) of the capacitance that is applicable for all time periods of the power pulse to ensure maximum capacitive discharge energy, limited by the highest charge and discharge voltages and therefore having minimum energy losses per time unit. There has been little efforts to improve the relaxor material properties ( W rec andη ) which are quite dominated by the contribution of their bulk properties, especially their inherent huge electrical polarizability along with stable intrinsic intrinsic polarization which are driven by the local disorder that introduces non-uniform interactions allowing their complex polar relaxation behaviour. Recent research have therefore focused more on developing various nanostructure in order to externally improve dielectric and energy storage performances while the role of local disorder and compositions induced by inhomogeneous distribution or different occupations of structural sites within the relaxor bulk has often been less considered, with an exception made by controlling the amorphousness of the nanodomains as evidenced by large temperature spans for changes occurring below 10 nm and corresponding to nanometric structural transformations strongly related to certain recoverable capacitances particularly contributing to capacitive pulse energy storage. This paper focuses the optimization of such structure leading to dielectric capacitors providing large energy storage capacitance of 15.2 J cm-3 and highly coupled efficient energy storage efficiency of 91% through a synergistic design of materials made possible by optimised control of heterogeneous local distributions of elements within a solid solution (heterolocating elements inside nanostructure domains leading to clustering) which has been modelled here through a atomistic structural model elaborated using the (reverse Monte Carlo) RMC method associated to multi-scale model made possible by integrating several characterizations results including (neutron and X-ray) scattering. The cations from the modifiers at sub-nm length scale form clusters which incorporate distinct ionic displacements towards lower remnant polarisation according to simulations performed within the framework of the density functional theory and which finally make for the optimum structural factor of complex perovskite dielectric capacitors according to the reported combined increase of (a) high polarisability at high field; (b) small remnant polarization contributing to efficient saturation along the maximum polarizability direction and low energy dissipated through the bulk contribution within the space vector potential due to low irreversible polarization; (c) the built-in field, namely high breakdown field induced from the beneficial polarisation factor and contributing to reduce more externally losses through charging by lower leakage currents contributed from lower disorder within bulk and at interfaces between grains which inhibit further polarization variations above their saturation limit even in presence of large charges. The optimized relaxor structural arrangement enables a superior dielectric performance provided over large temperature span (stable efficiency) as well as capacitances exhibiting high instantaneous capacitances at pulse polarisation reversal during charging/discharging as well as also less capacitive dispersion throughout current discharge especially occurring between maximum currents of a pulse and thus having higher values at peak current pulses when resistance is smallest therefore highest capacitances at instant of maximum current have to be sustainable in order to make up for discharge capability throughout the complete duration of the pulse current flow and associated stable overall energy density to match requirements at full efficiency for achieving high value of the energy stored within pulse capacitances.
